title: Negative slope
text:
Goal: Given a function, how can we find it’s minimum value
Let x be a vaiable with can take any value from -inf to +Inf. We can think of x as the x axis. Now let y = x^2.
This means that for any value of x (say 2) we just double it to get y. (2 * 2 = 4).
y = 2x is rule we have defined. We can also call it a function (y is a function of x; as value of y depend solely on the value of x).
We know x looks like the x-axis, but how does y look. lets plot it.

import torch
import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px

x = torch.arange(-100, 100+1, 1)
y = 2 * x 

px.scatter(x=x, y=y)








Its a line which is going upwards as x increases!
Now lets think why that the case. Lets investigate a few values of x and they corresponding y values.

y_func = pd.DataFrame({
    "x" : x,
    "y" : y,
})

y_func["x change"] = y_func["x"] - y_func["x"].shift(1)
y_func["y change"] = y_func["y"] - y_func["y"].shift(1)

y_func.loc[y_func["x"].isin(range(0,11))].tail(10)








x
y
x change
y change

1
2
1.0
2.0


102
2
4
1.0
2.0

2.0

2.0


110
10

1.0
2.0







In our table above if x1 = 5 & x2 = 6, y1 = 10 and y2 = 12. This implies that if we move one unit along x axis (6-5), we automatically move 2 unit along upwards along the y axis. For each point we can see that if the value of x increases by 1, the value of y increases by 2.
Let’s see why that happens. Consider 2 values of x. - x = x1, then y = 2 * x1 - x = x2, then y = 2 * x2 - Here x2 > x1
Then, - Increase in x = Distance moved along x-axis towards the right - Increase in x = x2 - x1 - Increase in y = Due to movement in x axis, how much did we move along the y-axis (upwards) - = y2 - y1 = 2 * x2 - 2 * x1 = 2 * (x2 - x1)
Notice that we moved along y-axis 2 times of how much we moved along the x axis! More importantly, we did not specify what is the difference between x2 and x1. This implies that for any increase along the x-axis towards the right we will always move 2 time upward along the y axis.
If x = 6.0 and x2 = 6.1; y1 = 6.0 * 2 = 12.0 and y2 = 6.1 * 2 = 12.2 - Increase in x (movement along x axis going from x1 to x2) - = 6.1 - 6 = 0.1 - Increase in y (movement along x axis going from x1 to x2) - = 12.2 - 12 = 0.2 = 2 * 0.1 = 2 * increase in x
This is why y = 2x is a line! And, the line “grows” by a factor of 2!
Why is it a line: > Because y moves up by the same factor for every movement to the right. Since this factor is always the same, the graph never bends and forms a straight line. In the example below we will see what happens when the slope is not constant
Growth factor of 2:
> As we take a step towards the right of x axis, we move 2 times as much upward along the y axis. Also, this is irrespective of how big a step we take towards right side of x-axis.
Also, if we keep increasing x the value of y keeps increasing. This is why we see y = 2x growing upwards as we move towards the right of x axis.
Hence, growth can be defined as \(\text{Growth or Slope} = \frac{\text{increase in } y}{\text{increase in } x}\) The above formula is intutive. For a line, how fast it grows depends on how quickly y increase when x is moved by a little bit to the right of x-axis. If y increase too much for a small change in x, the line will be steeper.
Hence, increase in y (rise)/ increase in x (run) gives us a constant factor for a line. Higher the value of factor (slope) steeper the line.

Negative slope
What would a negative slope imply? Its actually quite simple.
The negative sign indicates that the line goes downward as the value of x increase.
Lets consider another function: z = -2x

x = torch.arange(-100, 100+1, 1)
z = x * -2

px.scatter(x=x, y=z)









z_func = pd.DataFrame({
    "x" : x,
    "y" : z,
})

z_func["x change"] = z_func["x"] - z_func["x"].shift(1)
z_func["y change"] = z_func["y"] - z_func["y"].shift(1)

z_func.loc[z_func["x"].isin(range(0,11))].tail(10)

We observe that: 1. Now the value of y decreases by a factor of 2 as the value of x increases 2. Considering any 2 value of x in the table, slope = -2 Hence, negative slope indicates negative growth (as x increase, y decreases) and the slope tells us how fast the decrease is (steepness of decrease in the value of y)

x = torch.arange(-100, 100+1, 1)
z = x ** 2

px.scatter(x=x, y=z)








It looks like a curve! One intution for the curve is that as the value of x gets large the square of the number grows much more than the number smaller to it. Hence, the “growth” is not the same for larger numbers

example = pd.DataFrame({
    "x" : x,

example["Difference"] = example["x"] - example["y"]
example.tail(10)

title: About
text:
About Me
Hi! I’m a data scientist who enjoys understanding how things work, especially when it comes to Machine Learning, AI and technology. I started this blog as a place to document my learning journey and share concepts in a simple, intuitive way. The goal is not just to memorize formulas, but to build a deeper understanding of the ideas behind them.   Whether you are learning these topics for the first time or revisiting them after years, I hope this blog helps make complex ideas feel a little more approachable.

title: Understanding Slope
text:
Let \(x\) be a variable that can take any value from \(-\infty\) to \(+\infty\). We can think of \(x\) as the x-axis.  Also, we define: \(y = 2x\).  This means that for any value of \(x\), we simply double it to get \(y\). Example: if \(x\) = 2 then \(y\) = 2\(x\) = \(2 * 2\) = 4.






Note



\(y = 2x\) is a rule we have defined.  In other words, \(y\) is a function of \(x\) because the value of \(y\) depends entirely on the value of \(x\).


We know what \(x\) looks like, it is simply the x-axis. But what does \(y\) look like? Let’s plot it.

import torch
import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px

x = torch.arange(-100, 100+1, 1)
y = 2 * x

px.scatter(x=x, y=y, height=300).show()

                            
                                            


 Now let’s think about why that is the case. Let’s investigate a few values of \(x\) and their corresponding \(y\) values. 

y_func = pd.DataFrame({
    "x" : x,
    "y" : y,
})

y_func["x change"] = y_func["x"] - y_func["x"].shift(1)
y_func["y change"] = y_func["y"] - y_func["y"].shift(1)

print(y_func.loc[y_func["x"].isin(range(0,11))].tail(10))

      x   y  x change  y change
101   1   2       1.0       2.0
102   2   4       1.0       2.0
103   3   6       1.0       2.0
104   4   8       1.0       2.0
105   5  10       1.0       2.0
106   6  12       1.0       2.0
107   7  14       1.0       2.0
108   8  16       1.0       2.0
109   9  18       1.0       2.0
110  10  20       1.0       2.0


 In our table above, if \(x_1 = 5\) and \(x_2 = 6\), then \(y_1 = 10\) and \(y_2 = 12\). This implies that if we move one unit along the x-axis (5 to 6), we automatically move 2 units upwards along the y-axis.
Let’s see why that happens. Consider two values of \(x\).

If \(x = x_1\), then \(y = 2 \times x_1\)
If \(x = x_2\), then \(y = 2 \times x_2\)

Here, \(x_2 > x_1\). Therefore,

Increase in \(x\) = Distance moved along the x-axis towards the right

Increase in \(x\) = \(x_2 - x_1\)

Increase in \(y\) = Due to the movement along the x-axis, how much did we move upwards along the y-axis?

\(= y_2 - y_1\)
\(= 2x_2 - 2x_1\)
\(= 2(x_2 - x_1)\)


Notice that we moved along the y-axis 2 times the distance we moved along the x-axis!
More importantly, we did not specify what the difference between \(x_2\) and \(x_1\) is. This implies that for any increase along the x-axis towards the right, we will always move 2 times upward along the y-axis.
For example, if:

\(x_1 = 6.0\), then \(y_1 = 6.0 \times 2 = 12.0\)
\(x_2 = 6.1\), then \(y_2 = 6.1 \times 2 = 12.2\)

Increase in \(x\) (movement along the x-axis from \(x_1\) to \(x_2\)): \(= 6.1 - 6.0 = 0.1\)  Increase in \(y\) (movement upwards along the y-axis from \(y_1\) to \(y_2\)):

\(= 12.2 - 12.0 = 0.2\)
\(= 2 \times 0.1\)
\(= 2 \times \text{increase in } x\)

This is why \(y = 2x\) is a line! The line “grows” by 2 for each 1 unit increase in x.






Note



Why is it a line?
The rise (increase in \(y\)) is always the same multiple of the run (increase in \(x\)) for any pair \((x_1, x_2)\) where \(x_2 > x_1\). As the ratio of the rise to the run stays constant, the line never bends. In the next section, we will see what happens when the slope is not constant.








Note



Slope of the line  As we take a step towards the right along the x-axis, we move 2 times as much upwards along the y-axis. Also, this is independent of how big a step we take towards the right side of the x-axis.  If we keep increasing \(x\), the value of \(y\) keeps increasing. This is why we see \(y = 2x\) growing upwards as we move towards the right along the x-axis. 
Increase in \(y\) (rise) denoted as \(\Delta y\)  Increase in \(x\) (run) denoted as \(\Delta x\)  \[ \text{Growth or Slope} = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}\]
The above formula is intuitive. For a line, how fast it grows depends on how quickly \(y\) increases when \(x\) is moved slightly to the right along the x-axis. Therefore, the slope or growth is constant for a line (for \(y = 2x\), slope is 2). The higher the value of slope, the steeper the line.



Negative Slope
What would a negative slope imply? It’s actually quite simple.  The negative sign indicates that the line goes downward as the value of \(x\) increases.  Let’s consider another function: \(z = -2x\)

x = torch.arange(-100, 100+1, 1)
z = x * -2
px.scatter(x=x, y=z, height=300).show()

z_func = pd.DataFrame({
    "x" : x,
    "z" : z,
})

z_func["x change"] = z_func["x"] - z_func["x"].shift(1)
z_func["z change"] = z_func["z"] - z_func["z"].shift(1)

print(z_func.loc[z_func["x"].isin(range(0,11))].tail(10))

                            
                                            


      x   z  x change  z change
101   1  -2       1.0      -2.0
102   2  -4       1.0      -2.0
103   3  -6       1.0      -2.0
104   4  -8       1.0      -2.0
105   5 -10       1.0      -2.0
106   6 -12       1.0      -2.0
107   7 -14       1.0      -2.0
108   8 -16       1.0      -2.0
109   9 -18       1.0      -2.0
110  10 -20       1.0      -2.0


We observe that:

z decreases by 2 for every 1 unit increase in x
Considering any 2 value of x in the table above, slope = -2

Hence, negative slope indicates negative growth (as x increase, z decreases) and the slope tells us how fast the decrease is (steepness of decrease in the value of z)
